title: Sri Lankan Kottu Roti

ingredients.name: godamba roti or parotta, torn into strips
visual_checks: Use 4–5 store-bought layered parottas torn into irregular strips for convenience, or make from maida, salt, oil, and water (see substitutions).
ingredients.notes: Use 4–5 store-bought layered parottas torn into irregular strips for convenience, or make from maida, salt, oil, and water (see substitutions).

subs.name: 4–5 store-bought layered parottas or leftover chapatis torn into strips — this is the fastest and most practical shortcut used in home kitchens.

ingredients.name: chicken breast or thigh, thinly sliced

subs.name: 250 g extra-firm tofu, pressed and cubed, then fried golden before adding — makes an excellent vegetarian kottu.
Skip the egg entirely for a no-egg version and add 1 tbsp extra soy sauce for depth.
Bean sprouts or thinly sliced green beans add a similar crunch and colour.

ingredients.name: green chillies, finely chopped
visual_checks: Reduce to 1 for a milder plate.
ingredients.notes: Reduce to 1 for a milder plate.

ingredients.name: soy sauce
visual_checks: Adds a deep savoury note and Sri Lankan street-food character.
ingredients.notes: Adds a deep savoury note and Sri Lankan street-food character.

steps.text: Season the chicken slices with a pinch of curry powder and salt. Set aside. If using store-bought parottas, tear or cut them into roughly 2 cm irregular strips. If making roti from scratch: mix 300 g maida, 1 tsp salt, 1 tbsp oil, and enough water to form a soft dough; knead 5 minutes, rest 20 minutes, roll thin, cook on a hot tawa, then cool and tear into strips.

Heat 2 tbsp oil in a kadai or wok over high flame until it shimmers. Add the sliced onion and stir-fry for 2 minutes until softened and beginning to colour at the edges. Add ginger-garlic paste, curry leaves, and green chillies, and stir for 30 seconds.

Add the chicken slices and the remaining curry powder. Toss and fry on high heat for 5–6 minutes, stirring constantly, until the chicken is cooked through with a little colour on the edges. Push everything to the sides of the kadai.

Drizzle the remaining oil into the centre of the pan. Crack in the eggs, scramble quickly with a spatula for about 30 seconds until just set but still soft, then fold the chicken and onion mixture back in and mix everything together. Add the shredded cabbage and capsicum strips.

Add the roti strips and pour in the soy sauce (if using). Toss everything vigorously on high heat for 3–4 minutes, using two spatulas to chop and fold the roti into the mixture. The roti edges should crisp slightly and pick up the curry-soy colour.
Taste and adjust salt. Remove from heat and serve immediately — kottu is best eaten piping hot while the roti strips still have some bite. Serve with extra green chilli and a wedge of lime.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: maida → 4–5 store-bought layered parottas or leftover chapatis torn into strips — this is the fastest and most practical shortcut used in home kitchens.; chicken → 250 g extra-firm tofu, pressed and cubed, then fried golden before adding — makes an excellent vegetarian kottu.; egg → Skip the egg entirely for a no-egg version and add 1 tbsp extra soy sauce for depth.; cabbage → Bean sprouts or thinly sliced green beans add a similar crunch and colour.
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Use store-bought layered parottas (available frozen in South Indian supermarkets) to skip the roti-making step entirely. The stir-fry technique is identical to Indo-Chinese fried rice — just swap rice for roti strips. A kadai over a high gas flame replicates the tawa environment well. Ceylon curry powder can be replaced with a blend of garam masala and 1 tsp dark-roasted coriander powder.
